danger_signals.meaning: Imminent death if player doesn't escape; escalating threat with acceleration

danger_signals.visual_form: Sprite collision with enemy or projectile
danger_signals.meaning: Instant death (one-hit-kill mechanic unless jumping on enemy head)
Fall damage = instant death; spatial danger from level geometry
Ranged danger; same lethality as melee contact

danger_signals.visual_form: Solid-color silhouette enemies in Ligar's Castle final area
danger_signals.meaning: Visual obscuring of enemy form increases threat perception; harder to distinguish attack patterns

safety_signals.meaning: Score tallying and checkpoint; temporary respite from danger

safety_signals.visual_form: Stones spawning from ground; dropped items with distinct icons
safety_signals.meaning: Temporary ability enhancement: Sun Power (overhead attack), Crown Power (multi-kill), Cross Power (invincibility), Tiger Power (jump-kill), Star Power (range extension)
Delay fire monster spawn; prevents forced-failure state
Temporary zone control; clears all on-screen enemies

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Safe zones at level ends; progression through distinct themed regions (Suero Mountain, Gran Mountains, Indora, Den of Sagila, Palace of Dorago)

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Dorago (boss entity); Palace of Dorago as seat of power
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Dorago appears as shadow version in Ligar's Castle; shadow rendering obscures true form
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Palace of Dorago as final area; presence of shadow monsters indicates faction territory

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Varied sprite designs across level types
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Unused dragon enemy, butterfly, robot, and spider enemies indicate faction diversity; shadow palette applied to Ligar's Castle enemies for visual unity
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Enemy density and type vary by region; final palace contains shadow-rendered variants

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Standard colored enemy sprites; accessible level geometry; generous continue-on-death mechanics
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player can lose all lives and insert coins to continue from furthest point; lower skill requirement

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Ligar's Castle introduces shadow-rendered enemies (single-color silhouettes); more complex level layouts
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Game-over is permanent—no continue option; forces skill mastery; palette obscuring increases difficulty perception

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Fire monster spawns; on-screen presence grows more aggressive
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Monster accelerates toward player over time; forces constant movement and evasion; signals failure state imminent

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Temporary ability enhancement; visual changes to attack patterns (Sun Power overhead swing replaces circular attack)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Extended combat range, multi-enemy kills, invincibility, or jump-kill capability; loss on death signals power expiration

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Same sprite/level layout as other regions
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Programming error caps max Tone/Last at 1023 and max hitpoints at 8 (vs. 4095/12 in other versions); final areas become exponentially harder; mechanical difficulty increase without visual signaling

environmental_storytelling.example: Suero Mountain (Primeval Mountain in JP), Gran Mountains (Valley of Rosa), Indora god rooms, Den of Sagila, Palace of Dorago—progressive geography from wilderness to evil stronghold signals narrative escalation toward final confrontation

environmental_storytelling.technique: Enemy sprite design and palette variety
environmental_storytelling.example: Early levels feature colored, distinguishable enemies; Ligar's Castle uses uniform shadow palette to indicate faction territory and create visual confusion (obscuring enemy form to increase threat perception)
Palace of Dorago uses shadow-rendered enemies and distinct visual style to signal villain's inner sanctum; unused dragon and boss-variant enemy designs reinforce oppressive atmosphere
End-of-level safe zones create breathing points in otherwise lethal environment; their presence and structure reinforce progression milestones
Power-ups emerging from stones and defeated enemies signals world-state rewards; frequency and type of items vary by region and progression stage

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Aggressively pursues player; accelerates over time; forces evasion and signals imminent failure state
One-hit-kill on contact; exceptions: jumpable for stun effect; defeat drops power-ups or bonus items
Appears as silhouette; true form hidden by palette; fight mechanics obscured by visual effect
Immediate death on enemy contact; can attack with Diskarmor (yo-yo mechanic); can jump on enemy heads for stun; climbing ropes available in some areas
Temporary immunity to all damage; visual change likely present but not documented
Data suggests dragon would sit in pit and rise with mouth open; robot and spider intended for late-game areas

notes: Rygar NES manifests world-state primarily through level progression architecture and enemy density escalation rather than explicit visual UI systems. Danger communication relies on high-difficulty spike mechanics (one-hit-kill, time pressure, permanent death in late game) rather than visual warnings. The shadow-rendering technique in Ligar's Castle is the most deliberate world-state visualization, using palette obscuring to create psychological threat escalation. Regional programming differences (European version cap at 1023 Tone/8 HP) represent invisible mechanical difficulty that contradicts visual parity—a failure to communicate altered world-state to player. Power-up system provides temporary safety through gameplay mechanic enhancement, not sustained worldbuilding. Faction identity is minimal: Rygar vs. Ligar opposition is narrative-thin in NES version, relying on final palace architectural distinction rather than visual faction markers throughout the game.
